data.title: FTAI Aviation: A Multiyear Compounder
data.description:
FTAI Aviation (FTAI) is one of those insanely good business models that you just own, put away, and let it compound for you. Even though the stock has more than doubled from last year's lows (which I'll address), future growth will justify further stock gains in my opinion.

Basic Business

FTAI was started by Fortress Investment Group about 15 years ago. Its initial business was acquiring, leasing, and managing transportation and infrastructure assets, including aviation equipment like aircraft and jet engines. After it spun off its other infrastructure assets into FTAI Infrastructure (FIP) in 2022, which I wrote about several weeks ago, it became a pure-play aircraft and jet engine leasing and services business. While the leasing business is steady Eddie, the services piece is by far the most interesting and evolving. I'll address the leasing portfolio first before I move on to the various services.

Legacy Leasing

As you can see below, the company's balance sheet was comprised of more planes than engines. The balance sheet has pivoted far more to engines and has shrunk in overall size.

The leasing business has enjoyed tailwinds of lower plane production by Boeing stemming from long-standing manufacturing problems by Boeing that were particularly centered around the 737-max lines. It has also benefitted from technical problems with the LEAP engines, the next generation jet engine successors to the CFM56 engines, which CFM (a JV between GE and Safran) stopped producing in 2019-2020. Fewer narrow-body planes and problematic next-gen engines have made existing inventory more valuable and allowed rate increases as old leases expire. Therefore, whereas the legacy leasing balance sheet has declined, the adjusted EBITDA from that portfolio has done well.

As you can see above, the leasing business has also benefitted from gains on sale and insurance recoveries. These gains have been somewhat controversial, which I'll address below.

More important, last year, the leasing business gained a new segment, SCI (Strategic Capital Initiative). This is an awesome business that I will address next.

SCI

Engine and aircraft leasing is generally a steady revenue business. You get some tape bombs occasionally from broken contracts with bankrupt lessees, etc, but the planes are usually easily moved to other customers, and overall it's a 10-12% ROE business (with obvious variability). However, it is balance sheet intense.

FTAI devised a better, higher return structure for the leasing business.

Key details of the SCI I structure are below:

  • Final amount: $2.0 billion (upsized from $1.5 billion due to strong investor demand).
  • Closing: Fundraising completed on October 27, 2025.
  • FTAI's co-investment: Approximately 19% (FTAI invested alongside third-party investors).
  • Total purchasing power: Over $6 billion, including ~$4 billion in debt financing (backed by commitments from ATLAS SP Partners / Apollo and Deutsche Bank).

SCI I's strategy is to acquire mid-life, on-lease narrow-body aircraft (mainly Boeing 737NG and Airbus A320ceo). At about $13-15 million per aircraft, the company expects to acquire about 300-375 planes in this first vehicle by the end of this quarter. The first 45 planes of SCI I came from FTAI's leasing portfolio. This was done at market pricing and was responsible for much of the gain on sale FTAI recognized in 2025 (market value of the planes they sold to SCI was higher than book value).

SCI I was so successful in fundraising and aircraft purchases that FTAI announced SCI II on the Q4 conference call. An anchor equity commitment from a major investor is already secured, providing a strong starting point for another $2 billion of equity.

FTAI will earn management and incentive fees on SCI as well as the pro-rata returns on its equity stakes. I could see FTAI continuing to sell planes from its leasing portfolio in SCI and thereby converting its leasing business into a much higher return on equity asset management structure.

The MRO Business

The biggest change in FTAI's business mix has been the development of the MRO (Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul) business. As the owner of a large fleet of aircraft, FTAI created its own MRO division. It specializes in refurbishing CFM56 and V2500 engines.

FTAI wasn't content to just maintain and repair the engines. They formed a JV with Chromalloy, a leading independent provider of aftermarket services for gas turbine engines, which has obtained PMAs (Parts Manufacturer Approval) for four key parts of the CFM56 engine. A PMA is granted by the FAA. It allows a company that is not the original engine manufacturer to manufacture and sell aircraft engine parts. OEMs earn fat margins on replacement parts for their engines. PMAs allow FTAI to sell "generic" aftermarket alternatives to these OEM parts.

PMAs are not easy to get. The parts go through rigorous testing and certification to meet the same safety and performance standards as OEM parts. Once you have a PMA, however, you can charge less for the parts than OEMs but still earn great margins.

The Chromalloy JV has four PMAs:

    1. HPT (High Pressure Turbine) Nozzle Guide Vane
    2. HPT Shroud
    3. LPT (Low Pressure Turbine) Stage 1 Nozzle Guide Vane (including an “up-change” version)
    4. HPT Stage 1 Blade

A fifth PMA application is under review.

Many MROs earn mid-teen to mid-20% EBITDA margins. FTAI's MRO business earns upwards of 40% EBITDA margins thanks to the PMAs

FTAI has made another innovation that gives it a structural advantage over other MROs. Because it has such a large footprint and can manufacture its own parts, it has a large inventory of spare whole engines or modules (CFM56 are comprised of 3 modules). If an airline needs an engine repaired, FTAI doesn't say, "Ok, send us the engine and we'll fix it" like other MROs. FTAI says, "Give us the broken engine or module and a pile of money, and here's a brand new engine or module." The difference means no downtime for aircraft versus weeks of downtime while the other MROs fix the engine or module. The airlines don't care if they have an original engine or module any more than you care whether your car has an original or rebuilt engine. You only care that it works and you can drive your car.

FTAI calls this model MRE (maintenance, repair and exchange). It allows them to charge more because airlines have no downtime. Combined with the PMAs, it pads FTAI's margins. The combo allowed them to grow their Aerospace Products EBITDA 71% last year.

Naturally, FTAI has maintenance deals with all SCI planes. Most engines need full or near-full repair/overhaul every five years. As SCI scales, the number of engines/modules feeding into this system becomes very large. 19% of MRE contract revenue was from SCI in Q4 '25, $107mm. That was probably $40-45mm of EBITDA, and that's with SCI I skill in the scaling phase.

Check out these slides that visualize the growth rate for the MRE business.

The number will continue to grow in 2026. As you can see in the slide below, the company is expecting to service 1050 modules (3 modules per engine in 2026) up from 757 in 2025. With the new fan blade PMA, I believe that the company makes about $1.1 million of EBITDA per CFM56 module. Unfortunately, you can't just multiply $1.1 million by 1050 since V2500 engines are in that math and are about $2.5mm per engine rather than $3.3mm. I still get about $1.05 billion of aerospace products 2026 EBITDA, up from $671 in 2025. This right on top of guidance. For 2027, adding 70 planes from SCI alone (no third party market share gains) adds about $250 million of EBITDA.

As the business grows, the company invests to add capacity. The company has been aggressive about adding service capacity and has invested in the balance sheet to provide exchanges. Despite this, the company generates $724 million of free cash in 2025 on $1.191 billion of EBITDA. That figure is after $240 million invested in SCI but also includes $54 million of insurance claims from lost Russian assets.

Power Generation

In December, the company announced a new initiative that very much surprised most people, including me. They started a division converting retired CFM56 jet engines into power generation (primarily for data centers). Access to power is the major constricting factor for data center development. There is a five-year waitlist for most natural gas turbines. The company can deliver these and have them running in two weeks.

A converted CFM56 can generate 25MW. The company believes it can sell 100 units in 2027 at about $25mm per unit with at least 40% EBITDA margins. To be clear, these were engines that were too old to put on planes. But they could be rebuilt for mobile power. This is essentially $1 billion of EBITDA in 2027 from assets that were essentially worthless.

Valuation

The company has guided 2026 EBITDA of $1.625 billion and $915 million of free cash flow generation, which includes $190 million co-investment in SCI II and ~$100 million in FTAI Power working capital/inventory build. I believe there is at least 10% upside to the EBITDA number, with about 80% of that dropping to free cash. I think the company can generate about $1.8 billion of EBITDA in 2026. $1.1 billion from aerospace products, $550 from leasing and $150 from SCI. That number grows to over $3 billion in 2027 when power generation kicks in and SCI II ramps and potentially $3.5 billion in 2028. The free cash flows at those levels get fairly silly. The company said the 2026 EBITDA guide would mean $1.2 billion of free cash without the inventory build for power and the SCI investment. That's about a 75% conversion rate.

At just $4 billion of equity, SCI should throw off about $60 million of management fees. I believe the equity returns for SCI are supposed to be north of 20%. Between SCI I and SCI II, the company will have about $780 million invested when they are fully ramped. That can add $156 million annually at just 20% returns. Plus there are incentive fees on the rest of the equity.

Here is the valuation based on 2026 EBITDA and cash flow guide, and I add where I think 2027 can be.

Market Cap @$251/shaer $25.789 billion
Debt $3.449 billion
Cash

$300 million
Enterprise Value $28.937 billion
EV/2026 EBITDA $1.625 billion 17.8
FCF Yield $915 million 3.5%
EV/2027 EBITDA $3 billion 9.6x
FCF Yield 2.25 billion 8.7%

Skeptics will argue that power generation will only be a five-year business as large gas turbine backlogs clear. I disagree. But even if they're right, 2500 MWs of installed power is a drop in the bucket of what's needed right now. The company can install 100 systems multiple years in a row. If they do that through 2030 and then the business stops, it's still a $10-15 billion opportunity and a significant portion of EV.

On the MRO/MRE front, competitors like Heico (HEI) trade at 27x current year EBITDA. If we apply that multiple just to the 2026 guide for the MRO business, we get the current EV.

SCI seems like it can be an $8 billion AUM business by 2028. That would throw off $120 million in management fees alone. Midpoint valuation for private equity firms is about 10% of AUM. I believe SCI should garner a higher valuation since it would require far less staffing than a typical private equity firm and therefore equal higher margins. Either way, you get this and the leasing business for free if you apply comp.

Adding this altogether, I think this stock can double over the next 12-24 months. It is a large moat business with a powerful flywheel.

Skeptics

Last year, the stock fell victim to some bogus short reports. Most involved questions around how the company accounted for intracompany pricing of maintenance between the MRO and the leasing company. They weren't questioning that the EBITDA was there, just that it was misallocated to the MRO instead of the leasing business. There were also questions as to the switch from lease accounting to industrial accounting and why the company chose to change auditors. Lastly, there were questions as to whether the company would be able to raise the money for SCI and deploy the capital.

All of the naysayers were proven wrong. Most importantly in my mind, the company generated a ton of cash even after making large investments. You can fake a lot, but cash flow is the hardest thing to fake in my mind.

Risks

There are a few risks here. One, another pandemic or something else shuts down or slows down world air travel. This is an aircraft-centric business. Any disruption to air traffic hurts it. There is also the risk that the power generation business falls flat. I think the company has a good line of sight to those first 100 installs, or it would not have put out that number for 2027. It is an unproven business, though, and could have problems. Lastly, use of CFM56 engines could drop, which would hurt current capabilities. However, management has mentioned that the next-generation LEAP engines could be an even bigger opportunity.

Conclusion

Management has done a great job taking an ordinary aircraft leasing business and converting it into a virtuous cycle business that feeds itself and even monetizes its end-of-life assets. Instead of a low ROE leasing business that is relatively easily replicated, it is a high ROE, large moat business that leverages several different capabilities. We are early on the growth curve here, but the line of sight to what the business could be is excellent. A tripling of EBITDA is possible here over the next three years. That kind of growth combined with high cash conversion should trade at higher multiples in my mind.

data.title: Genuine Parts: 69-Year Dividend King, Time To Buy At Historic High Yield
data.description:
Genuine Parts

Genuine Parts currently has a dividend high yield easily beats its competitors, as shown in the chart below

$Pr/Sh Current = US $ Price/ share currently for April 12, 2026

E Divi 2026 = Estimated yearly dividend payment for 2026

Div % Yield = % dividend yield / current price and estimated dividend.

Stock Company $Pr/Sh E Div Div %
Ticker Name Current 2026 Yield
(GPC) Genuine Parts 109.79 4.25 3.87%
(ORLY) OReilly 94 0.00 0.00%
(AZO) AutoZone 3514.47 0.00 0.00%
(AAP) Adv Auto Parts 56 1.00 1.77%

I have written previous articles about Genuine Parts (GPC) over the past two years.

In both cases, I liked the company and suggested waiting for a better yield and just holding on if you own it. I have held on, and now this update is to indicate that with the high historic 4%+ yield, it is time to buy.

Genuine Parts Company Description

Genuine Parts distributes automotive and industrial replacement parts, operating in 3 segments: North America Automotive Parts Group, International Automotive Parts Group, and Industrial Parts Group. The company was incorporated in 1928 and is headquartered in Atlanta, Georgia.

Much of the following information comes from Yahoo Finance, April 14, 2026.

The company distributes automotive replacement parts, accessories, tools, equipment, and related solutions for hybrid and electric vehicles, trucks, buses, motorcycles, farm equipment, and heavy-duty equipment along with specialized services, such as paint mixing, hydraulic hose assembly, battery testing, and key cutting. In addition, it provides independent repair shops and auto care centers under the NAPA brand while offering technical expertise and training programs to customers.

It also offers a wide range of bearings, seals, and gaskets; hose, fittings, hydraulics, and pneumatic components; abrasives, adhesives, sealants, and tape; pumps and power transmission; tools and testing equipment; electrical supplies and safety products; and chemicals and janitorial supplies.

Additionally, it offers inventory management and specialized repair services."

Also from Yahoo Finance News Summary, April 14, 2026, it is mentioned:

AI and Digital Transformation: The company is integrating AI into its operations, including expanding a robotics pilot and launching an internal AI assistant, as it seeks to modernize its processes."

From the company website, April 13, 2026.

NAPA = National Auto Parts and Auto care centers which are in over 6000 global locations. It currently has 9800 stores with 6700 being independent and 3100 company owned. The real claim to fame are its private label quality parts offered to its commercial customer and professional mechanics that are needed for auto dealers servicing bays.

Motion is its industrial parts group distributor, the 2nd largest, is very well known and highly regarded for its strong supply chains and quick delivery. It has 80 distribution centers in North America.

In total it has over 10,800 locations spanning the 17 countries as mentioned above. It has a smaller presence in Asia/Pacific operating under the brand of Repco."

SPLIT Announced February 17, 2026

Per the company website press release:

Genuine Parts Company (NYSE: GPC), a leading global service provider of automotive and industrial replacement parts and value-added solutions, announced its intention to separate the company into two independent, publicly traded companies.

1- Automotive Parts Group ("Global Automotive")

2- Industrial Parts Group ("Global Industrial").

The transaction, which is targeted for completion in the first quarter of 2027, is expected to qualify as a tax-free transaction for U.S. federal tax purposes to Genuine Parts Company shareholders.”

Split Transaction Details state there are no changes to the GPC executive team. The company names, executive teams and Boards of Directors for Global Automotive and Global Industrial will be announced at a later date.

The transaction is expected to be completed in the first quarter of 2027, subject to satisfaction of customary conditions, including final approval from the GPC Board and filing and effectiveness of a Form 10 registration statement with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission. The separation does not require shareholder approval."

7 year FAST Graph

The chart below shows 4 years of known statistics and 3 years of dotted-line estimates.

The black line in the chart is the price, and it rose nicely for quite some time but now has taken a trip down, revealing the undervaluation. The dark green area is the earnings, and the price rarely dips down that low. The last 2 years have been difficult for it with hate for gas vehicles, but that has changed, and the future looks to show rising estimated earnings with 5% for 2026, 8% for 2027, and double-digit 12% for 2028.

The current P/E is 14.38x and the normal blue line P/E is 17.27x, showing the low valuation. The price did get down under $100 and is rising once again but could as yet have another dip; no one knows. The best part is that the earnings are starting to rise, and so should the price.

The next earnings report will be April 21st, and Yahoo Finance News suggests today that earnings should be up 8% to $1.89. Up is good, and it is close to the estimate as shown in "FG" which would be $7.75 for the year or ~$1.93, making it very close.

Dividend King

It has paid a continuing rising dividend for 69 years, making it a Dividend King.

It pays on a JAJO (January, April, July, and October) schedule. It went ex-div on March 6th for payment on April 2nd with a dividend raise from $1.03 to $1.0625, or 3.15%. The 5-year DGR is 5.5%, so the raise was a bit lower than normal but did coincide with the lower earnings over the last few years and was to be expected. There are 2 more payments as yet for 2026 and then a repeat early in January.

Positive News

Yahoo Finance News Summary from today mentions:

Genuine Parts' shares rose 5.5% after billionaire Seth Klarman's Baupost Group increased its stake, indicating institutional confidence despite recent struggles."

Negative Head Winds

The only real issue might be competition from e-commerce and perhaps China for those who can wait to obtain needed parts. It should be noted that AutoZone, Inc. (AZO) and O'Reilly Automotive, Inc. (ORLY) have stronger retail supply chain models, while GPC has a better, faster, and more desirable wholesale model.

Rose Take and Recommendation

GPC is quality and has had a reliable, safe, rising dividend for 69 years along with an investment-grade S&P credit rating, which is now BBB-. The price has already been under $100 this year, which actually was lower than the drop to $104 in April 2025. The historic high yield now is the time to buy, and in full disclosure and total confidence, I did add some. Morningstar analyst has a $132 fair value, Yahoo Finance has $139.71 for 1 year out, while Value Line suggests $143 as a 2027 mid year price point, which points to capital gains too! Anything can happen, and if it were to go lower in price, I have confidence it can recover and would consider getting more. My price target is $125 before 2027 and maybe $140 or higher for 2028.

Competition

O’Reilly Automotive Inc.

Current price: $94

This is an auto parts retailer for “DIY” clients and provides aftermarket tools, parts, supplies, and associated equipment for service providers. It was founded in 1957 and has 6000 stores in 48 states, Puerto Rico, Mexico, and more recently Canada. It is headquartered in Springfield, MO.

Morningstar analyst gives it a wide moat.

Statistics cited primarily for P/E are from FAST Graphs a paid subscriber service I use.

No dividend.

It has a current P/E of 30.66x and a normal of 26.37x. Which makes it somewhat overvalued using that metric. It does have positive earnings.

The "SA" forward P/E is 28.89x, making it more attractive yet still pricey for me and a Rose Recommendation.

5 year Seeking Alpha chart

AutoZone Inc.

Current price: ~$3500.

AutoZone, the largest specialty retailer of DIY “do it yourself” parts in the US, was founded in 1979. It has 7140 outlets in the US, Mexico, Puerto Rico, Brazil, and the British Virgin Islands. It is headquartered in Memphis, TN. It has a market cap of ~$53 billion, and Morningstar analysts give it a wide moat for its business. It does not pay a dividend.

5 year Seeking Alpha chart

It has a current P/E of 23.27x and a normal of 19.66x. Which makes it somewhat overvalued using that metric.

Rose Recommendation is to watch and buy when and if the price gets lower and down to a more normal P/E, which it has been in the past.

Advance Auto Parts Inc.

Current price: $55

Primarily a US auto parts aftermarket product provider for DIY and professional installers. It was founded in 1929 and now is headquartered in Raleigh, NC. It has ~4935 stores in the US along with owning the Carquest brand in Mexico, Cayman Islands, Turks & Caicos, and the British Virgin Islands. It purchased the Die-Hard brand from Sears and advertised it by backing the 2020 film with that name starring Bruce Willis.

It has seen some real poor last few earnings years with bloated expenses and supply chain inefficiencies. It added a new CEO in 2023 to hopefully lead a turnaround. It had $13.04 in earnings back in 2022, and it dove down to -0.3 in 2024, and thus the dividend cut. It has gotten back to positive earnings estimated to be $2.76 in 2026. It has a current P/E of 27.79x, which is in line with the other competitors, as the price has risen despite the same $1.00 dividend since 2024 and probably no rise coming anytime soon.

5 year Seeking Alpha chart

Rose Recommendation is to avoid it, as I don't see a great future for a reliable, safe dividend or for much, if any, capital gains. Not worth a 1.8% dividend yield. It should be noted that it just had an S&P credit rating lowered from BB+ to BB, another reason to avoid it.

Summary/Conclusion

The Rose's Income Garden "RIG" portfolio is up 5.98% YTD and carries a 6.15% forward yield, so you know it has other very lucrative investment choices in it. My portfolio is primarily a dividend growth income provider and consists of 74 investments. GPC, as mentioned, is reliable and provides strong income, and bought at 4% historic high yield makes it a good candidate for capital gains as well, and hopefully future capital gains. Good investment choices and picking quality investments with rising value are some of the goals that will always be used for the portfolio.

data.title: Joby Aviation: Still Not A Buy Despite Robust Certification/Monetization Tailwinds
data.description:
I previously rated Joby Aviation, Inc. (JOBY) as a Hold in December 2025, given the nascency of its VTOL monetization prospects.

In this article, I shall discuss why JOBY's investment thesis has yet to improve despite the steep meltdown, given the management's underwhelming FY2026 guidance.

JOBY's Tailwinds and Headwinds Entering FY2026

Since my last Hold rating, JOBY has underperformed at -43.1% compared to the wider market at +0.3%, as similarly observed in its VTOL/helicopter peers in varying degrees.

Given their likely prolonged path to commercialization, pending FAA certification, it is unsurprising that these stocks have not been able to hold on to their prior H2'25 gains.

This is especially since the market is experiencing a rotation to value/dividend-oriented sectors, given the de-risking from the rising inflationary pressure and the prolonged Iran conflict.

1. Path to FAA Certification

For now, JOBY has highlighted their ongoing in-house tests of the "first FAA-conforming electric air taxi aircraft" on March 12, 2026, with it supposedly paving the path for "FAA pilots to start “for credit” Type Inspection Authorization testing" and commercialization in 2026.

Thanks to the Electric Vertical Take-off and Landing [eVTOL] Integration Pilot Program [eIPP], it is apparent that JOBY may enjoy an accelerated FAA certification process and eventually, "early operations this year in Arizona, Florida, Idaho, New Jersey, New York, North Carolina, Oklahoma, Oregon, Texas, and Utah."

Therefore, while these remain the early days to JOBY's eventual FAA certification in 2026, I am of the opinion that FY2027 may bring forth an excellent commercial monetization prospect.

This is especially since JOBY's Superpilot capability may also be embedded in future autonomous flight platforms, potentially in the defense sector, as observed in their ongoing partnership with L3Harris (LHX), an aerospace defense contractor, with further "operational demonstrations with government customers planned for 2026."

Given the increasingly volatile geopolitical environment entering 2026, the defense sector may very well be a growth driver over the next few years of elevated defense spending trends.

2. Expanded Partnership With UBER and Interested Countries

JOBY has also notably delivered on their promise of (eventually) integrating Blade's helicopter services into Uber's (UBER) ride-for-hire platform, as observed in the recent in-app preview for Dubai.

With flight testing/demonstrations already underway in Dubai and the management reiterating their plans "to carry our first passengers this year in the UAE as part of our 6-year exclusive access to the Dubai market," I am of the opinion that the company's commercialization ramp-up is likely to occur rather rapidly.

This is significantly aided by JOBY's new partnerships with numerous local operators in Saudi Arabia and customers in Kazakhstan, as the management secures their top-line growth prospects beyond the transitory FY2026.

3. Infrastructure Development and Pilot Training

Beyond monetization opportunities as discussed in segment 2, JOBY has also executed brilliantly through the ongoing developments of numerous Vertiport sites within the US and Dubai, among others, allowing them to rapidly offer their commercial services upon certification in respective countries.

The same has been observed in their soon-to-be-completed, FAA-qualified pilot training simulators, with it expected to train "up to 250 pilots a year" within a training period of six weeks.

These may "support high-volume commercial operations," with 10 pilots already "considered to be qualified."

These developments underscore JOBY's core competency across:

  • the design, engineering, manufacturing, and testing stages,
  • the expanded commercial/defense partnerships, and
  • the timely infrastructure/pilot training programs,

to deliver an accelerated eVTOL monetization trend moving forward.

4. FQ1'26 Preview

On the other hand, with JOBY set to report their FQ1'26 earnings call on May 06, 2026 (post-market), readers may want to temper their near-term expectations indeed.

This is because the management has previously offered an underwhelming FY2026 revenue guidance of $127.5M at the midpoint (+138.6% YoY), "with the vast majority generated by Blade."

This implies that JOBY does not expect the eVTOL operations to be materially accretive to their FY2026 numbers.

At the same time, their operating losses are likely to further widen, as observed in their H1'26 cash usage guidance at $355M at the midpoint attributed to the "core S4 certification and manufacturing readiness."

I expect JOBY to remain unprofitable on a Free Cash Flow basis as well, attributed to the management's plans to double their production capacity to four aircraft per month within the US in 2027.

These reasons are also why their debt reliance/dilutive capital raises are likely to remain a norm in the intermediate term, as last observed in January 2026, with up to $670M likely to be raised through convertible senior notes and up to $662M through the common stock offering.

While these have contributed to JOBY's rich balance sheet at $1.4B in cash/short-term investments as of FQ4'25 with an additional ~$1.2B received in February 2026, it goes without saying that long-term shareholders' equity continues to be diluted to 894.64M in FQ4'25 (+22% YoY), worsened by the growing SBC expenses to $127.88M in FY2025 (+22.4% YoY).

As a result, I urge readers to temper their expectations, since JOBY's investment thesis is unlikely to dramatically improve in the near term.

JOBY Remains a Hold at Best

For now, JOBY has breached the February 2026 trading floor of $9.40s, and it remains to be seen if the bulls may defend the current sideways trading along the $8s.

This is especially since JOBY's technical indicators imply its oversold position across the RSIs and trading volumes, with further underperformance more likely than not.

Particularly, despite the -59.3% correction from the 52-week highs and the mixed consensus forward estimates, JOBY remains expensive at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 60.72x against the prior highs of 6,954.71x.

Based on the Enterprise Value of $6.98B at the time of writing and the consensus FY2028 revenue estimates of $459M, the stock appears rather inflated at my estimated FY2028 EV/Sales valuations of 15.20x as well, against the current sector median of 2.16x.

When comparing JOBY's FWD EV/Sales valuations of 60.72x and my estimated FY2028 EV/Sales valuations of 15.20x to its direct helicopter/VTOL peers, including:

  • Archer Aviation Inc. (ACHR) at 145.71x/4.33x (using the same methodology),
  • Eve Holding, Inc. (EVEX) at 1,325.55x/1.39x,
  • BETA Technologies (BETA) at 57.49x/2.44x,
  • Strata Critical Medical (SRTA) at 1.09x/0.94x, and
  • Surf Air Mobility Inc. (SRFM) at 1.33x/0.80x, respectively,

It is apparent that JOBY remains inherently expensive at current levels, despite its supposedly accelerated eVTOL path to FAA certification and commercialization in 2026, against ACHR's target of 2028.

Pending further stock price moderation and/or accelerated revenue recognition over the next few years, I am of the opinion that JOBY is likely to underperform in the intermediate term, as similarly observed in the stock already losing all of their H2'25 price gains.

As a result of the minimal margin of safety from the expensive valuations, the likely to be delayed path to breakeven/profitability arising from their high-growth prospects, the potential shareholder equity erosion from further dilutive capital raises (or debt reliance), and the potential volatility from the elevated short interest ratio of 8.64%, it may be prudent to stay on the sidelines for a little longer.

JOBY remains a Hold (Neutral) here.

data.title: Hewlett Packard Enterprise: Too Much Demand Isn't A Bad Problem
data.description:
Regular readers of my articles know that I'm a tech-focused analyst. I have covered a variety of AI infrastructure companies, including chipmakers, networking providers, and those that offer server solutions. With that being the case, one stock that I might have overlooked is Hewlett Packard Enterprise Company (HPE).

As shown in the stock price chart below, one-year gains are a very healthy 74%, and there is little doubt that this has been driven by their significant exposure to AI infrastructure. Today, I have decided to initiate coverage by going over their most recent results that were reported just last month and looking at what the company had to offer at Nvidia's (NVDA) GTC.

Below, it is shown that their Juniper Networks acquisition is driving results in their networking segment. While overall normalized growth isn't the strongest right now, demand is shown to be quite robust. The company is supply constrained, and while that is a near-term risk to performance, such an attractive value proposition should bode well for the long term. Furthermore, guidance implies margin expansion, and their deepening partnership with Nvidia could be another catalyst. With the forward P/E still at a deep discount to the sector, I view Hewlett Packard Enterprise as a deep-value AI infrastructure play.

Top Line Results

Starting with their company-wide top-line results for FY2026 Q1, Hewlett Packard Enterprise seems to be seeing strong momentum in their business overall. The generation of $9.3 billion in revenue represents an 18% YoY increase. That is both a considerable acceleration of growth from the previous quarter and the year-ago quarter, so activity seems to be in a robust position. It should be stated, however, that these numbers include the Juniper Networks contribution, so in organic terms, results were less encouraging than what the headline numbers suggest.

We'll get into more depth regarding their segments just below, but there was quite a contrast in results between their Networking and Cloud & AI businesses. Do note that they reorganized their segments again for Q1 as they have consolidated their server, storage, and financial services businesses. Lastly, they actually still missed estimates by a significant margin of $46.71 million. All in all, while headline figures are encouraging, it will be shown below that they have been reliant on Juniper Networks for growth.

Segment Performance

For their networking segment, revenues grew by 152% in reported terms after including the Juniper Networks acquisition, but on a normalized basis, there was really just a 7% jump. Therefore, organic growth doesn't seem to have been overly strong. Nonetheless, as you can see in the breakdown above, data center networking is leading the way in terms of performance, and that's really no surprise as the demand for infrastructure solutions continues.

You can see above that operating margin has dipped quite a bit from levels seen in the year-ago quarter to show that profitability is on shaky ground right now. In my view, investors should give them a little more time since they're still in Phase 2 of their Juniper integration. Revenue and cost synergies will likely take some time to play out, so stronger numbers are expected down the road.

For their Cloud & AI segment, there was likely a bit of disappointment for investors. As a whole, there was a 3% decline in revenues to show that activity was soft. In the above breakdown you can see that their all-important server revenues declined. That said, we will take a look at some demand metrics for their AI solutions in particular next. As for operating margin, there has actually been some improvement YoY in this segment. This should help to calm some commoditization fears that have been caused by fierce competition in this space.

Demand Reasonably Strong

While AI orders of $1.5 billion in Q1 were lower than what we saw in the year-ago quarter, there still seems to be reasonably strong demand for Hewlett Packard Enterprise's AI solutions. As shown above, their AI backlog has grown from the previous quarter to a record $5.3 billion. With backlog now up around 71% YoY, the rate at which the company can convert this demand into revenue seems to be the larger concern than the strength of demand itself. We'll see in the guidance section later whether this will translate into stronger top-line growth moving forward. In their earnings call, it was reportedly mentioned that demand is indeed outpacing supply at this point, so there may be little the company can do to boost growth in the near term.

Profitability Quite Solid

While it was mentioned in the segment analysis that there was mixed margin performance, as a whole Hewlett Packard Enterprise saw 2.8 percentage points of operating margin expansion YoY. This shows that overall profitability and business efficiency are in a good place. As shown above, EPS growth was reported to be 33% YoY, and they also managed to beat bottom-line expectations by $0.06. The fact that they missed top-line estimates but still beat for EPS shows that margin was stronger than expected. Moving forward, investors should look for margin improvement in their networking segment as the Juniper acquisition gets better integrated.

Financial Guidance

Hewlett Packard Enterprise provided Q2 revenue guidance that was above consensus and EPS guidance that was in line with estimates. We'll focus on the full-year FY2026 outlook to see where things are going on the larger timeframe.

For revenue growth, they see 5% to 10% in normalized terms, which is a decent but still relatively sluggish growth rate, all things considered. While their Cloud & AI revenue guidance was lowered, they still expect mid- to high-single-digit growth in this segment and in their networking business (normalized). Therefore, moving forward Hewlett Packard Enterprise is expected to see solid if not extraordinary business activity. Recall from earlier that there are significant supply constraints now.

For non-GAAP operating profit growth, they are expecting a range of 32% to 40%, and that implies margin expansion. With Juniper cost synergies likely materializing soon, this is not a major surprise. Lastly, for adjusted EPS, the midpoint at $2.4 hints at a 24% jump from FY2025 levels.

GTC 2026

At GTC 2026, Hewlett Packard Enterprise deepened their collaboration with AI leader Nvidia. The company announced their Alletra MP X10000 and as a major vote of confidence, it is the first NVIDIA-Certified Storage object-based platform. Hewlett Packard Enterprise also expanded their NVIDIA AI Computing by HPE portfolio. President and CEO Antonio Neri stated that

The AI race is fundamentally about speed, scale, and trust. Our industry leadership across cloud, networking, and AI enables organizations to operationalize AI securely, efficiently, and at an unprecedented scale. Together with NVIDIA, HPE delivers turnkey AI factories and networks that transform AI ambitions into real enterprise value.

It is clear that the company is committed to offering state-of-the-art solutions that are also user-friendly and can add value quickly. While they are supply constrained now, this strong value proposition should drive long-term attractiveness and differentiation in a space that is packed with competition.

Valuation Is Cheap

While Hewlett Packard Enterprise's forward P/E doesn't seem too low relative to historic levels, note that as of this writing, Seeking Alpha shows that their valuation is at a steep discount to the IT sector based on a variety of multiples, including the non-GAAP forward P/E. In my view, despite some challenges they are facing, this price point may be attractive for investors.

In today's article, it was shown that the Juniper acquisition has driven their growth in Q1. While more organic growth would have been desired, demand is currently in a strong position as demonstrated by the record backlog. For the company, the greatest challenge, or risk if you will, is the supply challenges. With backlog soaring YoY to outpace revenue growth, Hewlett Packard Enterprise has been unable to truly channel all that demand into financial growth. Nonetheless, with collaborations like the one with Nvidia likely to drive long-term growth and margins expected to expand moving forward as per their guidance, the outlook seems to be decently solid.

Supply constraints are unfortunately limiting their growth, but at a forward P/E of just around 10, Hewlett Packard Enterprise is essentially a deep value play. At the end of the day, having products that are valuable to clients is probably the most important, as supply limitations are likely to ease somewhat at some point down the road. Too much demand to handle is usually a much better problem to have than too little, so the fact that the stock's valuation is severely discounted makes the risk/reward favorable.

My Take

Despite the stock having already surged 74% in the past year, I believe the stock could still be in for more outperformance moving forward. The fundamentals are mixed, but they don't need to be anywhere near perfect to make the risk/reward attractive, as the valuation gives investors a good margin for error. While supply constraint risks are not the most desirable, they're also not the most undesirable. The fact that they are selling out is a sign that they're doing something right, and in the long run that could be an asset. After today's analysis, I have decided to initiate Hewlett Packard Enterprise at a buy rating.

data.title: Otis: Finding Its Floor
data.description:
Shares of Otis (OTIS) have been lagging a bit in recent times, something that I attribute to demanding valuations from the get-go and higher interest rates causing an overhang on new construction activity across the globe.

This trend has continued for a couple of years and is seen continuing in 2026, and while overall sales growth is delivered on in a tough new equipment market, overall valuations look reasonable. However, I fear the quality of services, even as the company just made an interesting deal to improve service business and hopefully service levels.

Other, higher-conviction ideas, including recent M&A efforts, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

Growing Its Services

Otis announced that it has acquired a majority share in WeMaintain, a fast-growing and technology-enabled service company for the elevator and escalator industry.

CEO Judy Marks calls service the foundation of the industry, and innovation is key, with customers seeking greater reliability and technology in performance, and that is just what WeMaintain provides.

While no financial details on the deal have been announced, and the deal likely is a bolt-on deal, the operations are sizable at the same time. The company employs 350 workers and reportedly has raised tens of millions in funding.

Adding to a Solid 2025 Performance

By the end of January, Otis reported a 1% increase in 2025 sales to $14.43 billion, with constant currency sales being flat. The impact of higher interest rates having an impact on construction activity levels was clearly seen in the results.

Lucrative service revenues rose 6% to $9.44 billion; these were very lucrative sales with margins reported at a quarter of sales. New equipment sales were down 7% to $4.99 billion, as already low margins fell by more than a point to just below 5%. Of course, margins in this area of the business are not necessarily the goal, with the company selling equipment on the cheap to obtain lucrative services revenues for years thereafter.

The mix of growing sales in the more lucrative service revenues meant that adjusted EBIT margins improved by 40 basis points to 16.9% of sales. This allowed earnings to rise by 3% to $1.60 billion, as buybacks made it so that adjusted earnings were up by 6% to $4.05 per share.

Net debt is reported at $6.86 billion, yet with adjusted EBITDA seen near $2.5 billion, leverage ratios are seen in the high 2s. With the trends for 2026 seen as more of the same, the company guides for 2026 sales to advance by mid-single digits to $15.0-$15.3 billion. This is comprised of further declines seen in new equipment sales, offset by continued growth in service revenues.

All this should drive adjusted earnings per share to rise by mid- to high single digits. That more or less translates into a $4.25-$4.50 per share range, as additional earnings power keeps that leverage firmly in check.

Valuation Thoughts

Trading around the $80 mark, the company trades around 20 times trailing earnings and about 18-19 times forward adjusted earnings. This looks like a reasonable multiple, but real growth is difficult to find, as lower equipment sales do not bode well for future revenue growth in services.

However, while current growth is not too convincing, the business model is very sticky. This includes some 2.5 million units being maintained and some 2.5 billion people being moved daily, with the company having over 70,000 workers, of whom two-thirds are field professionals.

While new equipment sales are seen falling in 2026, the company actually reported a 2% increase in constant currency increase in new equipment backlog in the fourth quarter. A 30% increase in modernization backlog is spectacular, with the total backlog up to $18.4 billion, far outpacing reported sales, with more maintenance efforts benefiting from aging fleets.

What Now?

Frankly, I have only covered Otis back in 2020 when the company was spun off from its parent company, United Technologies. At the time, the company was a $13 billion business, which posted revenues in a 50/50 split between services and new equipment sales. Of course, new equipment sales have fallen substantially in absolute terms but also in relative terms, now making up just about a third of sales here.

Operating in a then $75 billion global industry dominated by just a few operators, thus an oligopolistic structure, the investment thesis and companies benefited from some long-term drivers. This includes critical building infrastructure, highly regulated operations, and a trend towards urbanization, making the long-term growth fundamentals look solid. The company employs the famous razor-blade model, as is very evident in the differentiated margin profile of new equipment and services.

Based on an estimation of corporate cost allocation, as well as minority interest in the business, I believed that the earnings power was seen around $2 per share at the time. Trading in the mid-forties, these shares traded around 22 times earnings, and in the 5-6 years that followed, shares have essentially doubled. Earnings have doubled as well, as multiples have come in a little to 18-19 times earnings.

That is quite an achievement, certainly, as new equipment sales have been trending lower for years. The company is not alone in this, as peers like Schindler and Kone are seeing similar trends here. Having peaked in the low $100s in 2024, Otis now trades around the $80 mark, levels at par compared to 2021, while some real profit inroads have been made.

Franky, I like the progress made by the business in a tough external environment, including falling equipment sales, with service revenues being impressive. Trading at a below-market multiple, there is much to like about the business and the share price here, if not for the concern about the services.

The focus on service revenues and fat margins is evident in some customer reviews, but that applies to the industry at large. However, the company recently discussed service revenue practices on its earnings call, indicating that it is doing pilot pricing in maintenance and repair. The company does this by adopting prices in relation to the value delivered to customers, with such practices seen boosting revenue growth by a point in 2026.

That sounds small but is a big achievement, yet it could backfire as well. The latest deal could fit right within this strategy or really help to improve customer service, as I would favor the latter.

In all this, I regard shares as reasonably valued and probably a decent long-term investment. This certainly is the case as new equipment sales recover, but I have some small reservations here on that topic.

data.title: 2026 Stock Market Outlook: Cycle Convergence & What's Next
data.description:
In our last broad market update, the S&P 500 was trading near 6,850, grinding through its fifth consecutive month of going nowhere. I drew a clear line in the sand at the 6,780 level. This was where the bulls needed to hold to keep the broader uptrend intact heading into 2026.

This level (SPX6780) remains of utmost importance for the bulls. If it breaks, then the period of volatility has already begun as we head toward 6500 – 6300 in the coming weeks. This will likely complete the first leg in a larger correction, as we mount a bounce that makes a lower high into later 2026.

That level clearly broke, dropping the broad market over 400 points, and finally bottoming at 6,316 on March 30th. We are now staging a bounce, which our analysis suggests will likely fail to make new highs, as the period of volatility that we have been flagging since November takes accelerates.

This should not be new to our readers. In our February 2026 report we pointed widening divergences across the Magnificent 7, deteriorating price action in non-tech sectors, and historically elevated bullish sentiment supported by record high margin debt. Each of these warning signs precedes periods of volatility, and all were present well before the index peaked on February 25th, 2026.

Since then, the evidence has continued to build. The dominant cycles that best correlate with 2026 are all pointing lower, which is being confirmed by a growing number of markets and sectors breaking critical support globally. The current bounce will likely draw investors back in, while the weight of evidence suggests a more cautious stance.

Our commitment is not to a single outcome; it is to follow the evidence wherever it leads. As of now, we are seeing the evidence suggest a multi-quarter topping process is finally starting to break lower, which should set up an excellent buying opportunity for those prepared.

Why Broad Market Deterioration Points to Extended Volatility in 2026

In our February report, we flagged that the S&P 500's push to new highs was not being confirmed by key markets beneath the surface. Most notably, the Magnificent 7, which collectively account for roughly 30% of the S&P 500's weighting, had already begun rolling over. This was substantiated by every single Mag 7 stock putting in a top between July and February, even as the index pushed to one final high on Feb 25th.

This kind of divergence, where the index makes a new high while its most dominant components quietly deteriorate, is a classic warning sign of a market in the late stages of a bull run.

The weakness, however, was not confined to technology. Financials (big banks) had also put in a notable top in early January, completing a full 5-wave advance off the April 2025 low, and subsequently making their first series of lower lows since that uptrend began. This suggested that weakness that started in tech in July of 2025 was spreading.

Since that report, the list of confirmed tops has only grown. Across multiple sectors and markets, we are seeing the same technical signature: a completed 5-wave advance off the 2025 lows, with the final 5th wave pushing to new highs on deteriorating volume and weakening momentum, followed by the first meaningful lower low since the uptrend began.

Small caps tell the same story. The IWM completed its advance off the April 2025 low with a final push on fading volume and momentum. It has since printed its first lower low since the bull run began, a meaningful structural shift for a segment of the market that typically leads both up and down.

Industrials have been a leading sector since the 2025 bottom. As you can see below, based on the above criteria, it is also confirming a period of weakness has likely begun.

What makes this picture more concerning is that this topping process is not a uniquely United States phenomenon, it is playing out globally. To name a few, The German DAX completed a 5-wave advance off its April 2025 low and has since recorded two consecutive lower lows.

The Canadian TSX traced the same pattern – a full 5-wave advance accompanied by weakening momentum, followed by a decisive shift in trend structure and its first lower low on elevated volume.

Taken together, the weight of this evidence points to two unsettling conclusions. First, the volatility we are currently experiencing is likely in its early stages, not a brief interruption of the bull market, but the beginning of a more sustained and complex corrective period.

Second, and perhaps more importantly, this is a globally synchronized topping process. When markets around the world begin rolling over in unison, each completing the same technical structure, each showing the same deterioration in breadth and momentum, it signals that the forces driving the correction are not localized.

This raises an important question: if this is the beginning of something larger, what is driving it, and how long could it last?

To answer that, we turn to cycles.

2026 Market Cycle Analysis: Gann’s 60-Year Great Cycle Meets the Presidential Cycle

As we move into April, we now have a full quarter of price action to analyze. The pattern that has emerged is relatively unique, which helps narrow down which cycles best correlate with 2026's market behavior, so far.

This year saw a top form within the first few weeks of the year, followed by a controlled yet choppy downtrend that bottomed into late March. The market is now staging a bounce.

When we overlay this specific price pattern against historical cycles, two important time periods stand out as the strongest matches converging simultaneously in 2026: the 60-year cycle, which Gann himself called the Great Cycle and considered the most powerful of all his time periods, and the 4-year cycle, widely known as the Presidential Cycle.

The fact that both cycles are converging in the same window is not something that happens often. In Gann's framework, the more cycles that align simultaneously, the more powerful and significant the resulting turning point. This confluence adds meaningful weight to our view that 2026 is setting up to be a challenging year, but also that the low forming this year may ultimately represent another incredible buying opportunity.

The 60-Year Cycle Great Cycle

W.D. Gann is widely considered the master of market cycles. His work was largely built on the rhythmic repetition of the 60-year Great Cycle. He observed that markets, economies, and even geopolitical events tend to generally rhyme across 60-year intervals. His view was that human nature, collective psychology, and the underlying forces driving economic expansion and contraction repeat themselves on this grand cycle, making it one of the more reliable long-term roadmaps available.

As you can see below, when we overlay the S&P 500's price trend from 60 years ago onto the current secular bull market, it shows a strong correlation to the general trend, and at times, a near-perfect correlation to specific price movements.

According to the 60-year cycle above, we should now be entering a period of heightened volatility. If we zoom into how this year is lining up with the 60-year cycle, we are seeing stark similarities, already.

The 4-Year Presidential Cycle

Markets also tend to follow a well-documented 4-year rhythm driven by the political and economic policy cycle of a US president's term. What the data consistently shows is that the 2nd year of this cycle is the weakest of the four, characterized by policy uncertainty, elevated volatility, and below-average returns.

Within each 4-year cycle, there is always a significant low that tends to launch a multi-year uptrend. This low consistently falls within year 2. Since 1950, the 2nd year of a president's term has produced the cycle low 56% of the time — 9 out of 16 election cycles. The market only posts a positive return in year 2 about 53% of the time, making it the riskiest year for investors within the four year cycle.

When we overlay the 4-year Presidential Cycle onto 2026's price action, the correlation to what we have seen so far is striking — a top in the early weeks of the year, followed by a controlled and choppy decline into late March, now setting up for a bounce into late April through early May.

The Composite Cycle Roadmap for 2026 Markets

If we combine both dominant cycles into a composite, we get a general roadmap for the general trend in 2026.

What stands out is how closely the price behavior we have observed so far - a controlled, overlapping decline into late March, now transitioning into a bounce - mirrors what both cycles would have predicted. If accurate, this bounce will likely draw many investors back in, and it is the kind of move that tends to create false confidence before the next leg of volatility resumes.

What makes 2026 particularly significant is that the 4-year Presidential Cycle low and Gann's 60-year Great Cycle are lining up at the same moment in time. In Gann's own words, it is at the simultaneous convergence of cycles, not any single one in isolation, where the most powerful and lasting market turning points are made.

Three Market Scenarios for 2026 — And What Would Trigger a Bullish Pivot

The famous economist, John Maynard Keynes stated, “It is better to be roughly right, than precisely wrong.” It is a sentiment that has been echoed by the world's great money managers across generations - the conviction to take a clear position, paired with the discipline to abandon it when the evidence demands otherwise.

That principle guides the thesis presented in this report. The weight of evidence, from completed topping patterns across diverse global markets and multiple U.S. sectors, to the convergence of dominant market cycles, points to the volatility we are currently experiencing as the beginning of a more sustained corrective period, not the end of one.

That said, markets do not always follow the most probable path. If the divergences we are tracking reverse and start making new highs, and/or if the cycles we are monitoring break their historical rhythm, we will update our analysis and pivot our stance accordingly.

How this can translate into a clear pivot can be found in the NASDAQ-100 (QQQ). If this index breaks out to new high and closes the week over these highs, this will be a clear signal that the market is shrugging off these warnings and likely mounting a push higher.

The NASDAQ-100, led by the Mag 7, have been leading this market down. It has been the weakest major index since the topping process began in late October. For this reason, if it can confirm a new high, and close over this high on a weekly basis, this will be the line in the sand that will warrant a pivot away from our defensive posturing.

When the facts change, we will change our mind. Until they do, we will continue to follow the evidence. Right now, the evidence points clearly to a multi-quarter topping process finally breaking lower, which for the patient and prepared, should set up one of the better buying opportunities this cycle has to offer.

How this looks on a larger time frame can be viewed below. Based on the price structure of the bull market off the 2022 low, two scenarios present themselves as the most probable paths forward.

  • Scenario 1 (Blue) — Wave 4 Correction

The current decline represents the early stages of a larger 4th wave correction. Under this scenario, the NASDAQ-100 finds its low in the $500 - $445 range, setting up a meaningful buying opportunity for a final 5th wave advance to new all-time highs in the coming year. This remains the primary count.

  • Scenario 2 (Red) — Wave 5 Top

What cannot be ignored is that all the necessary waves are already in place to complete the bull market pattern off the 2022 low. The overlapping swings and deep corrections throughout this advance are consistent with an ending diagonal pattern. Unfortunately, because the NASDAQ-100 is tracing an ending diagonal pattern, this determination cannot be made until we see a sizable drop and mount some type of bounce.

  • Scenario 3 (Green) – One More Swing into the Fall

Based on the weight of evidence, this is not my primary perspective. However, as stated above, the QQQs can close the week at all-time highs, this will become the primary perspective that we track. Here, the broad market will trend to new highs, likely on decelerating volume and momentum, completing a final 5th wave sometime into the Fall of 2026.

In conclusion, the evidence presented in this report did not emerge overnight. It accumulated gradually, across months and several markets, in the way that meaningful trend changes always do. From the Magnificent 7 rolling over well before the February peak, which has now spread to Financials, Industrials and Small Caps, to the synchronized topping patterns spreading across global markets, to the rare convergence of Gann's 60-year Great Cycle and the Presidential Cycle, the weight of evidence has been pointing in the same direction for some time.

That does not mean the path forward will be a straight line lower. Markets rarely are. The current bounce was anticipated, and it will likely do what bounces in corrective markets are designed to do — restore confidence, draw investors back in, and set the stage for the next leg of volatility. That is the nature of the environment we are navigating.

Our posture remains patient and defensive. Not because we are committed to a bearish outcome, but because the evidence has not yet given us reason to be otherwise. The line in the sand is clear. A weekly close at new all-time highs in the NASDAQ-100 changes the conversation. Until that signal arrives, the most probable path continues to favor a defensive posture.

Please note: The Tech Insider Network conducts research and draws conclusions for the Fund’s positions. We then share that information with our readers. This is not a guarantee of a stock’s performance. Please consult your personal financial advisor before buying any stock in the companies mentioned in this analysis.

data.title: loanDepot: Still Bullish On Financial Improvement Potential
data.description:
I'm keeping loanDepot, Inc. (LDI) as a Buy-rated name. I expect the company to record faster growth and turn profitable in FY2026.

My constructive view of real estate policy shifts and their implications for LDI was detailed in the previous Jan. 12 article.

Growth Momentum Can Be Sustained

The group unveiled "a strategic partnership with Figure Technology" (FIGR) to debut "express path home loan products" last Wednesday. This follows LDI's March 30 disclosure regarding the setting up of a "new home lending company serving West Texas buyers" with Betenbough.

I believe that the firm can build on its positive trajectory from late 2025 with the above-mentioned moves.

Loan throughput for LDI rose 23.1% sequentially and 11.9% YoY to $8,041M in Oct-Dec '25. That surpassed the management's $7.5B guide (midpoint) by +7%. I'm impressed this marks a 13-quarter peak.

The enterprise is seizing a bigger slice of the pie in the U.S. mortgage sector too. Its 4Q2025 market share was 1.4%, or a 20bps expansion versus the preceding three-month period. LDI is well-placed to make further headway, given the industry's unconsolidated state. It emphasized at the early-March analyst briefing that "no retail lender currently controls more than 5%."

My mid-Jan piece also highlighted its "in-house loan servicing model" which resulted in "low attrition and optimized marketing expenses." A measure tracking this strategy's success is the "Organic Refinance Consumer Direct Recapture Rate" or "ORCDRR." This metric improved from FY2024's 68% and 3Q2025's 65% to 71% for the final quarter of last year. I anticipate that the reduction will boost LDI's ability to secure more lending opportunities from the installed base.

ORCDRR's Definition

In my opinion, the company's future is bright with an expanded portfolio and new origination pathways.

According to the Apr 8 announcement, the latest "fully digital 5x5 HomeLoan" co-created by LDI/FIGR boasts "approval in as little as five minutes and funding in as few as five days." This puts loanDepot in a better position to meet demand from time-sensitive borrowers.

Also, I think LDI's recent collaboration with residential developer Betenbough could possibly be replicated to widen its sourcing network in the future. At the 4Q2025 results meeting, it stressed that "homebuilders bring new customers into our ecosystem across a diversity of transactions and geographies."

The sell-side is projecting that loanDepot's topline will accelerate from +12.2% for FY25 to +20.4% in FY26 as per S&P Capital IQ. I'm confident that it can achieve what the market expects, considering the growth engines outlined in this section.

Look Past Q4 Bottom-Line Disappointment

LDI is forecasted to be in the black this year with a net income of +$67M based on the consensus's estimates. I see good reasons to expect a turnaround.

I'm not worried about the worsening of its normalized net loss from 3Q25's -$2.8M to 4Q25's -$21.5M. That fell short of the analysts' -$7.7M prediction.

LDI's recent quarterly call commentary suggested that it "originated relatively fewer higher-margin second trust deeds" to "capture an increased share of refinance volume."

My take is that the company has intentionally sacrificed short-term margins to grow further in the refinancing-related segments. I assume this volume-first mentality should eventually translate into profitability enhancement over time through scale economies.

Also, the firm's early-March release disclosed "the launch of a new wholesale lending channel." It explained in the announcement that this will be a "new source of profitable growth" made possible by "leveraging the existing shared services infrastructure." It's clear to me that LDI desires to become more profitable by capitalizing on fixed-cost absorption in different ways.

Separately, I wrote about "AI-driven cost reductions" such as "the automation of LDI's call centers" in the Jan 12 update. I'm encouraged to learn that such efforts are beginning to bear fruit.

At its 4th quarter briefing, the group shared that "volume-related marketing and direct origination expenses were lower quarter-over-quarter despite higher volume." It didn't provide the specific cost breakdown. But LDI noted that this reflected "benefits of our investments in process improvements and technology initiatives."

You Shouldn't Ignore These Downside Factors

The potential synergies with Betenbough/FIGR might not be realized in full. This can occur if there are conflicts in management style between LDI and its partners.

loanDepot's future turnover may be weaker than what investors hope for. There could be a slowdown in the pace of market share capture assuming that its peers compete more aggressively.

My Closing Thoughts

I've remained a bull on this stock.

I currently value LDI at $1.92. This is derived by applying a peer-average 6.4x P/E to its consensus FY2027 EPS of $0.30. The listed comparables used for benchmarking were UWM Holdings (UWMC) and PennyMac (PFSI).

The company's shares offer an upside of +34% (1.92/1.43) based on the last done price of $1.43. I view the pivot towards positive earnings this year as a primary catalyst.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Big Banks Open Earnigs Season With Mixed Results
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Earnings season begins with mixed bank results and outlook divergence. (0:15) Producer prices rise less than expected. (1:05) Meta projected to surpass Google in global digital ad revenue. (2:02)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, earnings season kicked off in earnest with mixed results from the big banks.

JPMorgan Chase (JPM) trimmed its 2026 firmwide net interest income guidance, though Q1 earnings and revenue topped estimates, helped by broad business growth and a lower provision for credit losses.

Citigroup (C), by contrast, reaffirmed its 2026 NII outlook and also posted Q1 earnings and revenue beats, driven by strength in Markets and Wealth.

Wells Fargo (WFC) missed expectations on both NII and revenue. One analyst said a higher-for-longer rate environment should eventually stabilize net interest margins, but that improvement has yet to show up in the numbers and could weigh on the stock.

Outside the banks, Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) raised full-year guidance after better-than-expected Q1 results, supported by cancer drug sales, though revenue from its Crohn’s therapy continued to decline. The company also increased its quarterly dividend to $1.34/share from $1.30.

On the economic front, wholesale inflation in March came in softer than expected, potentially reflecting that the producer price index reference date was March 10 — before the sharp rise in energy prices.

The PPI rose 0.5% M/M, below the 1.2% consensus and matching February’s 0.5% gain. Annual inflation climbed to 4% from 3.4%, still under the 4.7% forecast.

Core PPI, which excludes food and energy, increased 0.1% M/M versus 0.5% expected and 0.3% prior. Annual core PPI rose to 3.8, below the 4.2% consensus.

Pantheon Macro noted that PPI trade services prices — the margin between distributor acquisition costs and customer prices — fell 0.3% in March.

However, the firm said those levels remain broadly in line with the 2024 uptrend and far above pre-Covid norms, reinforcing the view that retailers may have largely passed tariff costs on to consumers.

In other news of note, Meta (META) is on pace to surpass Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) in total digital ad revenue by year-end, according to Emarketer.

The Facebook parent is projected to generate $243.46B in net global ad revenue in 2026, up from $196.17B in 2025. Google’s ad revenue is expected to reach $239.54B in 2026, compared with $214.06B last year.

Meta’s share of global digital ad spend is forecast to rise to 26.8% in 2026, edging past Google’s projected 26.4%.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, Goldman Sachs’ equity team says companies tied to investment in power infrastructure remain an attractive secular growth theme.

Strategist David Kostin noted that future global investment in power infrastructure — potentially accelerated by conflict in the Middle East — could provide another tailwind. Unlike many other secular growth names, the group has sharply outperformed YTD.

Goldman highlights Caterpillar (CAT), Duke Energy (DUK), Cummins (CMI), Dominion Energy (D) and Array Technologies (ARRY).

See the whole list here.

data.title: Goldman Sachs: Cloudy Near-Term Outlook, Still Bullish Long Term
data.description:
Introduction

One of the largest financial institutions, The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS), reported their Q1 earnings yesterday and delivered what I thought was a decent report.

They managed to beat on both their top and bottom lines but saw their share price pullback briefly. In my opinion, this has to do with the high amount of economic uncertainty, particularly from the failed talks so far between the U.S. and Iran.

If the war continues, Goldman Sachs could see more volatility, potentially leading to underperformance after a strong rally in the past year.

In this article, I discuss their latest earnings, fundamentals, and why despite my Buy rating, GS could underperform in 2026.

Previous Buy Rating

I last covered The Goldman Sachs Group this past January in an article titled: Another Double-Digit Raise, Another Signal: Goldman Sachs Isn't Done Yet.

Since, the stock has underperformed, down over 9% compared to the S&P (SP500), down 2% over the same period.

Despite my bullishness, I did warn that their strong price rally put them at risk of a potential price correction as they offered downside to Wall Street's price target of $893.79.

During Q4, GS delivered a strong performance with their bottom line up 27%, indicating robust dealmaking. They also raised the dividend 12.5%, supported by strong growth in net earnings and net investment income.

This, their frequent buybacks, and top-tier balance sheet made me believe that their valuation could potentially re-rate around 20x in the next 1 to 2 years.

Latest Performance Showing Cracks?

As previously mentioned, the banking giant delivered a strong Q1 performance with a beat on EPS & revenue. Their bottom line amounted to $17.55, beating estimates by a sizable $1.16.

Their revenue beat was more modest, but a solid a $300 million, amounting to $17.23 billion. EPS jumped from $14.01 the prior quarter, while increased from $13.5 billion.

Year-over-year, both grew 23.15% and 14.1% respectively. For comparison, peer JPMorgan Chase (JPM), who reports Q1 earnings today (April 14th), is expecting EPS growth of 8.7% from $5.07 to $5.51 and revenue growth of 6.3% from $46 billion to $48.91 billion.

Goldman Sachs' strong performance was led by their Global Banking & Markets division, which saw 22% revenue growth from the prior quarter and 19% from the prior year.

But here's why I mentioned that GS' earnings may be showing cracks and likely why the price dropped after earnings. Despite the strong performance from their Global Banking & Markets division, their Asset & Wealth Management segment saw net revenues decline quarter-over-quarter.

Net revenues were down from approximately $4.7 billion to $4.1 billion. In the quarter, GS saw higher provisions for credit losses, which increased from $287 million to $315 million.

In their Platforms Solutions segment, net revenues were higher from the previous quarter, but significantly lower year-over-year. This was due to higher markdowns related to the Apple (APPL) card loan portfolio the company transferred to JPMorgan Chase.

These impact overall earnings and net investment income. And I believe this could be a sign of a slowing economy from surging inflation, an uptick in job losses, and higher for longer interest rates.

This also indicates potential financial pressure on large businesses, impacting lending conditions going forward. And could be why their net investment income missed the $3.67 billion consensus.

Nll amounted to $3.56 billion, down from $3.71 billion in Q4. Nll did increase from $2.90 billion the year prior, however.

All of these have resulted in a weaker consumer. And going forward, if the war continues, GS could see pressures increase further. If so, I suspect this could cause underperformance.

GS' balance sheet was in top-tier shape despite their CET1 ratio decreasing from 14.3% the prior quarter to 12.5%. This was a direct reflection of higher credit and risk-weighted assets. Buybacks also played a role, resulting in a 70 basis points decline.

But their CET1 ratio was still well-above the FED's 4.5% for banks. This is something investors should keep an eye on in the coming quarters as a decline could indicate a weakening economy.

Buybacks & Dividends

During the quarter, management repurchased $5 billion worth of shares. They paid $1.38 billion in dividends, well-below their net earnings of $5.63 billion.

Despite near-term volatility, net earnings grew a solid 19% year-over-year, indicating GS' dividends is safe amidst uncertainty. During the quarter, they returned $6.38 billion to shareholders.

Long-Term Picture Remains Intact

While I expect more volatility and potential underperformance in 2026 due to geopolitical tensions, I still believe Goldman Sachs could re-rate higher. And long-term investors should view the short-term volatility as a buying opportunity.

Currently, the stock trades at a forward P/E of 15.56x, above their 5-year average of 10.48x. But I think the last 5-years are misleading, mainly due to the pandemic and rising interest rates.

They also trade above their peers, JPMorgan Chase, Citigroup (C), and Morgan Stanley (MS). Morgan Stanley has a near identical forward P/E at 15.55x.

In my previous article, I stated I believe GS could re-rate to 20x earnings due to their strong growth. And once we see more economic clarity, likely in the next year or two, I foresee the share price expanding.

Analysts' are expecting strong growth in the next 3 years. As a result, strong price appreciation should follow. But near-term, I expect the share price to trade between $850 - $900 a share.

Over the next 2 to 3 years, I expect GS' share price to continue expanding and see above $1,000 a share. But the economic landscape remains a large risk.

The current maritime blockade will likely keep inflation high as oil prices will likely see volatility. If oil prices stay elevated as I suspect, then the overall market will likely underperform as recession risks increase.

Particularly, the banking sector could see higher amounts of volatility as financials could deteriorate because of higher credit losses, lower loans, and increasing risk-weighted assets.

Investor Takeaway

For investors looking at Goldman Sachs Group as an investment, it's important to have at least a 2-to-3-year outlook. Near term, I expect the stock could underperform due to higher amounts of economic uncertainty.

If the war with Iran continues, then higher amounts of inflation and growing recession risks will likely continue to weigh on the share price, resulting in underperformance.

But with strong, double-digit growth expected over the next 3 years, I expect Goldman Sachs' multiple to expand to around 20x. Backed by a top-tier balance sheet and strong fundamentals, I continue to rate The Goldman Sachs Group a Buy.

data.title: Amazon Could Explode Higher After Earnings
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) stock finally broke out of its 200 DMA last week, a key level that I had highlighted in my previous post. This was after management released their Shareholder Letter that contained three metrics that investors absolutely loved.

First, AWS’s AI revenue run rate has surpassed $15B in Q1 2026 three years into the AI wave. This is 260x larger than the revenue rate that AWS had attained three years after its commercial launch.

Second, the annual run rate for their chips business (inclusive of Graviton, Trainium, and Nitro) is now over $20B, with management hinting that they could be selling racks to third parties in the future.

Third, the management reiterated their capex plan for 2026 at $200B, but what mattered was the transparency in the monetization timeline that management laid out between 2027 and 2028.

The release of the Shareholder letter was also well timed with Anthropic’s (ANTHRO) announcement of its ARR reaching $30B, officially overtaking OpenAI (OPENAI). The reason it matters for Amazon is because Anthropic is the largest customer of AWS, meaning Anthropic’s explosive revenue growth directly translates into increased cloud spending, along with validating its custom silicon and strengthening the Bedrock (Models) layer. Not to mention, Anthropic’s surging valuation is also great news for Amazon, given that the latter has invested a total of $8B into the AI lab.
