In my previous post, I had described Amazon’s price action as similar to Google’s (GOOG) in 2023, where Amazon’s fierce push of its custom silicon roadmap is starting to look a lot like Google’s vertical integration strategy, with its TPUs (Tensor Processing Units) that it built to optimize its own services such as Search, YouTube, etc., while bypassing the “Nvidia tax” (NVDA) to drive superior price-performance. The latest revenue numbers from its chip business and management commentary from Amazon’s Shareholder Letter just validated my point earlier.

In this post, I will explain why Amazon looks primed to explode higher after earnings and how I am positioning myself, as it is my largest holding in the portfolio.

Amazon’s Shareholder Letter May Have Changed the Stock’s Destiny

Starting with AWS revenue, the segment grew 24% YoY with a $142B revenue rate, with AI revenue rate reaching $15B as of Q1 2026, three years into the AI wave, or 260x faster than the revenue growth that AWS had experienced since commercial launch.

What was also bullish was that management continued to point to the capacity constraint as the limiting factor to AWS’s revenue growth rate. In 2025, AWS added 2.9GW of new power capacity and is expected to double it by 2027.

A couple of weeks earlier, Amazon’s CEO Andy Jassy said that AI could help AWS achieve $600B in annual sales, double his prior estimate, while Goldman Sachs estimates that the two, Anthropic and OpenAI, could generate a combined AWS revenue of $13.2B in 2026, which could reach $60.1B by 2029.

For Q1 FY26, AWS is projected to grow its revenues by 28-29%, with no signs of slowdown at least over the next few quarters. What is also important to note is that with the acceleration in AWS revenue, its contribution to total revenue will grow. This is good news for the overall profitability of Amazon, especially as AWS is a significantly higher-margin business than its Stores business.

Particularly, if AWS indeed reaches the $600B revenue estimate by 2036 per Jassy’s projections, the total contribution of the segment to total revenue will reach close to 30% based on consensus estimates for revenue growth, up from 21.7% in FY25.

Assuming an operating margin of 35% for its AWS segment in 2036 and a 7% operating margin for its combined Stores business, including North America and International, operating margins could reach at least 16% by 2036, up from 11% in FY25. This is also a conservative estimate, as I expect its Stores business to ramp up profitability given the advancements in automation and robotics that the company is implementing.

Moving on to its chips business, management said that the annual revenue run rate (inclusive of Graviton, Trainium, and Nitro) is now over $20B and growing triple-digit percent YoY. Note that this is double the revenue rate from its Q4 FY25 earnings report of $10B.

In the Shareholder Letter, the management also said that

"if our chips business were a stand-alone business and sold chips produced this year to AWS and other third parties (as other leading chips companies do), our annual run rate would be ~$50 billion.”

What is extremely important to note is management’s emphasis on customers wanting better price performance, which marks a shift away from complete reliance on Nvidia’s chips towards custom silicon, where Amazon’s Trainium2 has about 30% better price-performance than comparable GPUs and has largely sold out, while Trainium3, which started shipping at the end of 2026, is nearly fully subscribed. Not to mention, AWS running its primary inference service on Trainium is also expected to help the company save “tens of billions of capex dollars per year” and provide “several hundred basis points of operating margin advantage."

Meanwhile, in the CPU space, Amazon’s Graviton also offers 40% better price-performance than other x86 processors and is now used by 98% of the top 1,000 EC2 customers.

Finally, when it comes to capex, management reiterated their capex commitment of $200B for the full year 2026. In my previous post, I had written that investors were growing skeptical of the elevated capex altering the free cash flow dynamics at Amazon, as its operating cash flow won’t be sufficient to fund the AI buildout like the previous years, forcing the company to tap into the debt market to finance its investment plans. At the same time, I also wrote about investors discounting the risk of failed or delayed revenue realization from the backlog, which would further strain margins and free cash flow.

However, in the Shareholder Letter, management made it a point to provide transparency into the monetization timeline of its capex investments from 2026 to take place between 2027 and 2028, while assuring investors that these investments are not being made on a "hunch."

At the same time, they also made their stance quite clear, saying that they are willing to endure short-term FCF headwinds for the “substantial medium- to long-term FCF surplus” and won’t be conservative in how they position themselves as a “meaningful leader."

How I’m Positioning Myself In This Bounce Higher

Amazon is expected to post its Q1 FY26 earnings on April 29, where revenue and earnings per share are expected to grow 13.74% YoY and 3.44% YoY higher.

On the revenue front, this would mark an acceleration for four quarters in a row, while an earnings per share beat of 140 basis points will also mark an inflection in the growth rate.

At this point, it is not just about beating the Q1 estimates but about forward expectations that will drive investor sentiment.

However, given the bullish tone that management sent in the company’s Shareholder Letter, I’m expecting much stronger than expected in Q1, along with highly favorable forward guidance for its AWS business, inclusive of its custom silicon.

In my previous post, I said the following:

“On a technical basis, if the stock is able to close above its 100 DMA at $227, it would likely head higher towards $240 per share. Whether it reaches an all-time high in the near term is difficult to say at the moment amid heightened scrutiny on AI capex as well as geopolitical uncertainty that has been plaguing the S&P 500.”

The good news is that Amazon stock superseded its 100 and 200 DMAs and is currently sitting close to the $240/share level that I had outlined above. What is also encouraging to note is that Amazon stock has been leading the “Magnificent 7” complex, like I predicted it would in this post, both on a 1M and YTD basis, as can be seen below.

Meanwhile, when looking at Wall Streets’ average price target of $280/share, there still is a potential upside of 17%+ from current levels.

Note that Amazon attaining $280/share would mark an all-time high in the stock price.

However, when looking at overall market conditions for the S&P 500 (SP500), we are approaching a key resistance level as the bounce is approaching its all-time highs set at the end of January. This is a level where I expect renewed volatility, especially as the macro narrative remains highly uncertain at the moment.

When it comes to my investment position in Amazon, I am continuing to hold the stock at current levels and into earnings but may consider trimming a small portion into strength and booking some profits, given my “overweight” allocation in the position.

Risks & Conclusions

The macro narrative is the biggest uncertainty at the moment, with no signs of resolution yet to the Iran war.

Meanwhile, until we see a peak of short-term supply-driven inflation, we are going to remain in the “higher for longer” narrative in the market, where every bounce tends to eventually fade away.

Particularly, when it comes to inflation expectations, Amazon’s Stores business, which accounts for close to 80% of Amazon’s business, could be negatively impacted from slowing sales and contracting margins.

While I’m delighted to see the move higher in Amazon’s stock following the release of the bullish Shareholder Letter and expect the strength to continue into earnings, I am also practicing the necessary risk management strategies with my position size until the macro noise clears.
